title: Defect Holonomy Near Rank-Deficient Mixed States
authors: Yu-Huan Huang, Xu-Yang Hou, Hao Guo
abstract: We investigate the geometry of mixed quantum states near rank-changing points, showing that these singularities function as effective geometric defects. The Uhlmann connection is well-defined only on the full-rank sector of the density-matrix manifold, while rank-deficient states form singular boundary strata where the bundle structure degenerates. By restricting to a punctured state manifold that excludes the singular set, we obtain a well-defined gauge structure and identify an asymptotically robust invariant: the Uhlmann holonomy around noncontractible loops encircling the defect. In an exactly solvable qutrit model, a restricted submanifold emerges on which the connection is locally flat yet carries nontrivial monodromy, analogous to flat connections with Aharonov--Bohm-type transport. The holonomy depends only on the ratios of the vanishing eigenvalues under frozen radial dependence of the eigenbasis geometry and a fixed angular loop. In contrast, the Uhlmann curvature may diverge path-dependently when eigenvalues shrink with distinct powers, with a leading spectral-prefactor scaling law, establishing that the holonomy survives as a universal asymptotic invariant while the curvature remains non-universal. Within the effective SU(2) defect sector, the conjugacy class of the holonomy, equivalently the Wilson loop variable, provides a continuous, non-quantized classification of the asymptotic monodromy surrounding the rank-deficient defect. This non-quantization does not imply a lack of robustness: the asymptotic holonomy is protected by the topology of the punctured manifold and is insensitive to smooth deformations of the loop or the radial profile.
summary: This work analyzes the geometry of mixed quantum states near rank-changing singularities, identifying these points as geometric defects. The authors show that while local curvature diverges path-dependently, the global topological invariant—the Uhlmann holonomy—remains robust. This holonomy provides a continuous classification of the defect, generalizing concepts from Berry phase physics to mixed systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates the geometric structure of mixed quantum states near points where the rank of the density matrix changes, treating these singularities as effective geometric defects.
main_result: The robust geometric information is encoded not in the path-dependent Uhlmann curvature, but in the global, asymptotically robust Uhlmann holonomy around the defect, which is classified by a continuous, non-quantized variable.
method: The authors use the Uhlmann connection and holonomy, generalizing Berry phase concepts to mixed states, by analyzing the topology of the punctured state manifold.
model_or_system: The analysis is performed on the manifold of mixed quantum states ($ho$), using an exactly solvable qutrit model ($	ext{SU}(2)$ sector) as a primary test case.
key_observables: Uhlmann holonomy, Wilson loop variable $W(C) = 	ext{Tr} U(C)$, and the Uhlmann curvature $\lVert F_U Vert$.
important_parameters: Spectral ratios of vanishing eigenvalues ($\zeta$), and the scaling exponents ($\alpha_a$) governing eigenvalue approach to the singularity.
main_assumptions: The analysis requires restricting to the full-rank sector and working on the punctured manifold $M^	imes$ to define the holonomy.
figures_summary: Figures schematically illustrate the punctured manifold $M^	imes$ and the non-trivial fundamental group $\pi_1(M^	imes) \simeq \mathbb{Z}$. They also plot the continuous dependence of the Wilson loop variable $W(C)$ on spectral ratios and the power-law scaling of the curvature magnitude.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the singularity at rank-deficient points, contrasts the path-dependent curvature singularity (Theorem 2) with the robust asymptotic holonomy (Theorem 1), and derives the continuous classification of the defect via the Wilson loop variable.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful geometric tool (holonomy) to characterize singularities in the space of quantum states that are robust against local perturbations, which is crucial for understanding quantum phase transitions or decoherence effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic illustration of the punctured manifold M× = M \ {ρ∗}. The removed rank-deﬁcient point ρ∗acts as a geometric defect, rendering loops encircling the puncture noncontractible. The three representative loops correspond to winding numbers n = 1, n = 2, and n = −1, which generate distinct homotopy classes in π1(M×) ≃Z.

Figure 2: Comparison between the Aharonov–Bohm eﬀect and the Uhlmann defect holonomy. (a) Parallel transport around a magnetic ﬂux tube in the Aharonov–Bohm eﬀect, illustrating the acquired geometric phase ΦB. (b) Paral- lel transport on the punctured mixed-state manifold M× = M \ {ρ∗}, where a loop encircling the rank-deﬁcient point ρ∗ acquires a nontrivial Uhlmann holonomy.
Figure 3: (a) Wilson loop variable W (C) = Tr U(C) as a function of the spectral ratio ζ. The red points mark the representative values ζ = 1, ζ = 7−4 √
